problem_summary: 2016 ICM Problem E (sustainability): analyze global water scarcity, distinguish physical vs. economic scarcity, and design an intervention plan for a chosen region. Team 52849 framed water scarcity as a "wicked problem" (no permanent optimum) and chose Haiti as a case of extreme ECONOMIC scarcity — the country has physical water but lacks money/infrastructure to clean, transport, and distribute it. The paper builds three interlocking models (a system-dynamics stock-and-flow model, a groundwater/cost optimization pipeline, and an agent-based social-network distribution model) and synthesizes them into a short-term (distribution via health centers) and long-term (piping/in-situ wells) intervention plan.
models_used: System-dynamics (stock-and-flow) model built in Vensim: water modeled as 6 coupled compartments (locked, available, potable, agricultural, industrial, residential) with a system of coupled ODEs for the flow rates (unlock, filter, funnel, runoff, transport, donate, corruption, waste, contamination)

Analytical reduction of the residential-water ODE by integration to a closed-form 'scarcity-driver' equation: drinking_water = r*e*A + t*(f*A + d*E - c), then sensitivity reasoning on which coefficients amplify (>1) vs. dampen (<1) the output

Custom raster groundwater-flow algorithm in Python: topographic map pixels -> hydraulic head via color lookup, then iterative diffusion-equation relaxation (n0 += k*sum of 4-neighbor head differences) to estimate annual water lost to the ocean; coupled with the water-balance identity Infiltration = Rainfall - Evapotranspiration - Runoff and runoff-coefficient based rainwater yield

Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO, plus a 'Repulsive PSO'/RPSO variant) to minimize total cost = treatment + collection + transportation over a 3-variable decision space (rainwater collection, well/groundwater collection, desalination) subject to demand and capacity constraints
Cost models: water-treatment cost curve (0.6/(x+0.04)+0.7 $/kgal as a function of MGD), pipe transport cost (2.31e-6 $/(gal*km)), groundwater (~$0.6), desalination ($1/kgal)

Agent-based social-network model in NetLogo: typed agents (common nodes, gangs, elites/government, health centers, distributors) on a spatial proximity graph with weighted links; water flows along edges proportional to link weight after each agent withdraws its 'demand'

Statistical inference layer over the ABM Monte Carlo runs: one-way & two-way ANOVA, Tukey HSD post-hoc tests, and unpaired t-tests (100 runs per condition) to show the proposed center-distribution method significantly beats elite distribution and is robust to population growth and node degree
Rawlsian 'maximin' fairness metric: a standardized root-mean-squared unmet-need statistic that up-weights the worst-off agents, used alongside mean-water-per-person to score distribution strategies
key_techniques: Decomposing one wicked problem into three model types matched to three sub-questions: supply/flow (system dynamics), cost-optimal sourcing (optimization), and last-mile distribution/equity (agent-based) — and explicitly stating which model answers which question

Turning a simulation model into an analytical insight: integrating the residential-water ODE to get a closed-form driver equation, then ranking interventions purely by whether a coefficient amplifies or dampens — yielding a defensible priority list (pipes > in-situ wells > personal filters > donations > anti-corruption > raising supply) without needing precise parameter values
Using physical scarcity vs. economic scarcity as the organizing dichotomy and proving Haiti is economically (not physically) scarce, which justifies the whole modeling direction

Encoding social structure as graph topology + edge weights (gang-gang strong, elite-common weak, distributor-common strong) so that 'corruption' and 'capture by elites' emerge from the simulation rather than being assumed
Embedding moral philosophy (Rawls' veil of ignorance / maximin) directly as a quantitative objective so the equity claim is measurable, not rhetorical
Quantifying the headline result — under elite distribution the common man receives 0.81 L of 30 L (~0.2%) — a single shocking number that motivates the intervention
Rigorous statistical validation of stochastic-simulation outputs (ANOVA + Tukey + t-tests with reported F, t, p), which is unusual and persuasive for an ABM
Anchoring the intervention in a real, named, evidence-backed organization (Partners in Health community health workers) so 'cultural adoptability' and self-sustainability are concrete
Building all three models to be region-agnostic / re-parameterizable, then specializing to Haiti — supporting the 'global analysis' part of the prompt
Sensitivity/robustness analysis per model (parameter perturbation for PSO, population growth and average-degree sweeps for the ABM)
why_it_won: Judge perspective: it nailed the ICM's interdisciplinary, real-world-impact mandate better than a typical optimization paper. (1) It diagnosed the problem correctly — recognizing Haiti's crisis is economic/distributional, not physical, which is the genuine insight the problem rewards. (2) It used three genuinely different and appropriate modeling paradigms (system dynamics, metaheuristic optimization, agent-based networks) and made them complement rather than duplicate each other, each answering a distinct question. (3) It produced concrete, actionable, culturally-grounded recommendations (piggyback water on Partners in Health's existing community-health-worker network short-term; pipes/in-situ wells long-term) tied to a real DINEPA plan — exactly the "implementable intervention" the sustainability problem asks for. (4) It backed claims with statistics (ANOVA/Tukey/t-tests on 100-run simulations) and a memorable quantified hook (0.2% reaches the poor). (5) It engaged seriously with ethics/fairness via a Rawlsian maximin metric, giving the equity argument quantitative teeth. (6) Strong narrative framing ("wicked problem," "Republic of NGOs," "From Riches to Rags") made it readable and credible to interdisciplinary judges while every modeling choice was justified with assumption+justification pairs and cited sources.
section_highlights: Structurally exemplary for ICM. Summary sheet front-loads all three models, the diagnosis (economic scarcity), and the concrete recommendation in one dense paragraph. A unifying "general metric" (success = (mi - ms - me) + a*s + b*c) is stated up front to tie money, sustainability, and cultural adoptability across all models. An interdisciplinary-relevance table maps disciplines (environmental science, economics, anthropology, epidemiology, history) to the problem. Every model section follows a disciplined pattern: motivation -> explicit Assumption/Justification pairs (each justification citing a source) -> equations/algorithm pseudocode -> "Applied to Haiti" -> sensitivity analysis. A dedicated "Comparison of Strengths and Weaknesses" section critiques each of the three models honestly. The narrative is genuinely engaging ("Once, it was the wealthiest colony in the world. Now... the poorest country in the western hemisphere") yet never sacrifices technical rigor. Figures carry quantified results (mean-water bar charts, distribution-method line charts) with ANOVA/Tukey/t-test statistics in the captions. Full runnable Python (groundwater flow, PSO/RPSO) and NetLogo source is included in the appendix, demonstrating the models were actually implemented.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Appendix code is buggy/garbled as transcribed: comparison operators render as semicolons (e.g. 'if i[0];bestParticleConfig', 'while iterations:10000'), a Python 'return' sits outside any function, and there are obvious typos (image color tuple (255,288,88) is invalid RGB, 'geang') — the implementations would not run as printed, so reproducibility is weak

PSO constraints and demand are essentially hard-coded for Haiti (rainMax, wellMax, demand=365*3*10^6) and the cost equation mixes magic constants; the '~$10M/year, rainwater is optimal' result rests on many uncited/loosely-cited approximations and is admitted to be only a lower bound (excludes capital and maintenance costs)

The groundwater 'diffusion' algorithm is a heuristic 4-neighbor relaxation on map-pixel colors, not a validated hydrogeology model; it assumes topography == hydraulic head and homogeneous isotropic soil, and no ground-truth validation of 'water lost to ocean' is provided

The agent-based model's headline numbers depend entirely on assumed link weights and demand ratios (gang>elite>common) that are asserted from narrative history, not estimated from data — the ANOVA/Tukey rigor validates differences between simulated conditions but not that the simulation matches reality

System-dynamics ODEs are written symbolically (no numeric parameter values, no actual Vensim time-series output shown), so the 'systems model' contributes a qualitative ranking rather than a calibrated forecast — the much-touted closed-form driver equation is derived under strong simplifications (waste constant, integration constant fixed)

Mean-water values are tiny and unit-ambiguous (0.081 vs 0.3 'L/person' from a 30 L injection across 100 nodes) — the absolute numbers are hard to interpret physically; the model is better at ranking methods than predicting real liters
Only one region (Haiti) is deeply analyzed despite the 'global' framing; the global claim rests mostly on the assertion that the models are re-parameterizable
Some figures reference statistics with placeholder/missing text ('??fill what we ran for degrees'), indicating last-minute assembly
reusable_patterns: Three-model division of labor for any resource/sustainability problem: (a) system-dynamics stock-and-flow for aggregate supply/flow, (b) metaheuristic optimization (PSO) for cost-optimal sourcing under nonlinear constraints, (c) agent-based network model for last-mile distribution and equity — pick models by sub-question, make them complement not duplicate

Integrate-the-ODE-to-a-driver-equation trick: reduce a system-dynamics model to a closed-form expression, then rank interventions by whether each coefficient is amplifying (>1) or dampening (<1) — gives a defensible priority list that's robust to unknown parameter values
Physical-vs-economic (or supply-vs-distribution) dichotomy as the diagnostic spine: prove which regime you're in first, because it dictates the whole solution direction
Encode social/political structure as a weighted typed graph and let pathologies (elite capture, corruption) emerge from simulation instead of being assumed
Operationalize a fairness principle (Rawlsian maximin) as a standardized RMS unmet-need metric so equity becomes a scored objective, reported alongside a mean/efficiency metric

Validate stochastic-simulation conclusions with proper statistics: run N=100 replicates per condition and report ANOVA F, Tukey HSD, and t-tests with explicit p-values rather than eyeballing bar charts
Assumption + Justification + citation triplets for every modeling choice — a compact, judge-friendly format that pre-empts 'why did you assume that?'
State one unifying success metric early (cost, sustainability, cultural-adoptability terms) and thread it through every model so the paper reads as one argument

Tie recommendations to a real named organization/program with evidence of effectiveness (here, Partners in Health community health workers) to make 'feasibility/cultural adoptability' concrete instead of hand-wavy
Lead with a single shocking quantified result (0.2% of distributed water reaches the poor) to motivate the intervention and make the paper memorable
Build every model region-agnostic and re-parameterizable, then specialize to one well-chosen case study — satisfies 'global + specific' prompts
Include a dedicated honest strengths/weaknesses section per model — signals modeling maturity to judges
